claim.content: The momentum-resolved valence-band dispersion of single-crystalline orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ at room temperature was measured by angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (ARPES); here the measured quasiparticle energy as a function of the in-plane crystal momentum was fitted near the valence-band maximum (VBM) by the isotropic in-plane parabolic dispersion E(k)=E_{\\mathrm{VBM}}+\\hbar^{2}k^{2}/(2m_{\\mathrm{exp}}), where k is the magnitude of the two-dimensional in-plane wave vector (units Å^{-1}), \\hbar is the reduced Planck constant, and m_e is the free electron mass, and the energy zero was set at the VBM defined as the energy of the maximum of the energy-distribution curve (EDC) at the band top; this fit, performed on the upper valence band along the surface high-symmetry direction labeled \\bar{\\Gamma}-\\bar{M}, returns an experimental hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e.

conclusion.content: The momentum-resolved valence-band dispersion of single-crystalline orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ at room temperature was measured by angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (ARPES); here the measured quasiparticle energy as a function of the in-plane crystal momentum was fitted near the valence-band maximum (VBM) by the isotropic in-plane parabolic dispersion E(k)=E_{\\mathrm{VBM}}+\\hbar^{2}k^{2}/(2m_{\\mathrm{exp}}), where k is the magnitude of the two-dimensional in-plane wave vector (units Å^{-1}), \\hbar is the reduced Planck constant, and m_e is the free electron mass, and the energy zero was set at the VBM defined as the energy of the maximum of the energy-distribution curve (EDC) at the band top; this fit, performed on the upper valence band along the surface high-symmetry direction labeled \\bar{\\Gamma}-\\bar{M}, returns an experimental hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e.

premises.content:
In an ARPES measurement of a crystalline semiconductor, let E(k) denote the measured quasiparticle energy as a function of the in-plane crystal momentum k (the magnitude of a two-dimensional wave vector measured in reciprocal angstroms, Å^{-1}), and let E_{\\mathrm{VBM}} denote the valence-band-maximum (VBM) energy defined as the highest occupied quasiparticle energy; within the momentum window used for fitting the upper valence band, taken here to be the region |k-k_{\\mathrm{VBM}}|\\lesssim0.1\\ \\text{\\AA}^{-1} around the momentum of the VBM, the quasiparticle dispersion is assumed to be well described by the isotropic in-plane parabolic form
      E(k)=E_{\\mathrm{VBM}}+\\frac{\\hbar^{2}k^{2}}{2m_{\\mathrm{exp}}},
      where \\hbar is the reduced Planck constant and m_{\\mathrm{exp}} is the fitted experimental hole effective mass; under this parabolic-band approximation, least-squares fitting of the experimentally extracted band maxima to this functional form returns m_{\\mathrm{exp}} and the systematic error in m_{\\mathrm{exp}} arising from neglected higher-order (nonparabolic) terms is smaller than the quoted experimental uncertainty of \\pm0.02\\,m_e, with m_e the free electron mass.

Let an energy-distribution curve (EDC) be the ARPES-measured photoemission intensity as a function of binding energy E at fixed parallel momentum k; the energy position of the maximum of the measured EDC at the band top is used to define the valence-band-maximum energy E_{\\mathrm{VBM}} for the purposes of setting the energy zero and extracting an effective mass, and any systematic shift of the EDC peak maximum from the true quasiparticle band-edge caused by many-body interactions, inelastic background, or the instrumental lineshape is assumed to be smaller than the quoted energy/mass uncertainty so that using the EDC peak maximum to set E=0 does not induce a systematic error larger than \\pm0.02\\,m_e in the fitted hole effective mass.

ARPES intensity maps measured over in-plane momentum (k_x,k_y) and as a function of photon energy (which probes different values of the momentum component perpendicular to the surface, k_{\\perp}) together with band-unfolding calculations show that the observed periodicity of four valence-band maxima at the surface Brillouin-zone corners and the absence of an additional valence-band maximum at the surface \\bar{\\Gamma} point are not caused by surface reconstruction, k_{\\perp} dispersion, or matrix-element effects; specifically, energy- and polarization-dependent ARPES measurements reveal no signature of an additional VBM at \\bar{\\Gamma}, and unfolding of orthorhombic bands onto the cubic cell shows negligible spectral weight transfer to backfolded bands, so that the measured dispersion used to obtain m_{\\mathrm{exp}} represents bulk-like valence-band behavior relevant to the orthorhombic structure.

steps.reasoning:
Define ARPES as angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy, and define $m_e$ as the free electron mass; state that the experiment measured the momentum-resolved valence-band dispersion of single-crystalline orthorhombic $CsPbBr_3$ at room temperature using ARPES with extreme-ultraviolet photons in the 20–40 eV range; note that samples were cleaved in situ under ultrahigh vacuum and measurements were performed at room temperature in the orthorhombic phase as confirmed by x-ray diffraction.
[28,29]

State that the valence-band photoemission intensity distribution was recorded as a function of energy $E$ and in-plane momentum wave vectors $k_x$ and $k_y$, and that the authors plotted cuts of this three-dimensional ARPES intensity to inspect the upper valence band dispersion along high-symmetry directions of the surface Brillouin zone.
Fig. 1(d)

Define "valence-band maximum" (VBM) as the highest-energy point of the valence band and state that the authors set the energy zero at the VBM; specify that the VBM energy was determined from the energy of the photoemission peak maximum in energy distribution curves (EDCs).
Fig. 1(e)

State that ARPES data along the surface-path $\bar{\Gamma}-\bar{M}-\bar{\Gamma}$ were acquired and that the upper valence band dispersion in this direction was extracted by fitting the valence-band energy distribution curves with a Gaussian line shape to determine the band maxima as a function of parallel momentum; define the symbol $m_{\mathrm{exp}}$ to denote the experimentally determined hole effective mass.
Fig. 3

State that the authors fitted the experimentally extracted band maxima of the upper valence band with a parabolic dispersion around the band maximum (i.e., they assumed $E(k)=E_{\mathrm{VBM}}+\frac{\hbar^{2}k^{2}}{2m_{\mathrm{exp}}}$ in the vicinity of the VBM, where $k$ is the in-plane wave vector measured from the VBM and $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant) and that the fit was performed on the upper valence band along the $\bar{\Gamma}-\bar{M}$ direction.
Fig. 3

Report the fitted numerical result: the parabolic fit around the valence-band maximum yields an experimental hole effective mass $m_{\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\pm0.02\,m_e$, where the stated uncertainty $\pm0.02\,m_e$ reflects the fitting and experimental uncertainties described in the methods and Supplemental Material.
Fig. 3
[30]

motivating_questions.content: For a quantitative assessment of polaronic mass renormalization one requires an experimental, momentum-resolved determination of the quasiparticle dispersion near the band extrema. Prior to this measurement, CsPbBr$_3$ lacked a direct ARPES-derived effective mass in its orthorhombic phase: optical and transport probes provided indirect or excitonic estimates and theoretical works predicted small bare masses, but none provided a momentum-resolved quasiparticle bandfit to benchmark many-body renormalizations. Without such an ARPES-derived $m_{\mathrm{exp}}$, it was impossible to establish unambiguously whether the experimentally relevant hole mass departs from DFT predictions and by how much, precluding an empirical test of polaron-based explanations for reduced mobilities and modified scattering in bulk CsPbBr$_3$.
